From: Michael Roberts  →  To: Vincent Devine
Date: 2024-12-19
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Thanks Vita. This is very helpful confirmation of what we [FCDO] understood the
position to be. | will keep you updated as and when | receive a name.

As | understand it, our Perm Sec is considering whether to proceed with seeking
agrement for the Head of Mission role alongside the security clearance process. If/if
the nominee is a member of the HoL, then it does make the process less bumpy.
Assuming the Government in question accept the nomination.

Gerard

subject: HMA Washington designate - vetting
text:
Email thread unit 5, message 1/1
Subject: HMA Washington designate - vetting
From: Rochelle Fisher  →  To: Gerard McGurk PERSONAL, JCS
Date: 2024-12-20
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE - RECIPIENTS ONLY

HiGerardand JCS *

The PUS’s office have authorised me to tell you that Lord Peter Mandelson has been

appointed by HMTK as the next HMA Washington. This appointment has not yet been

formally announced. | would be grateful if you can start the vetting process - | believe that as

member of the HOL, Lord Mandelson will not need DV but the role he will take up requires

STRAP. We would like him to take up post in January.

Lord Mandelson is aware that security processes will be needed. His email address is

Best wishes - and a very Happy Christmas to you both.

Rochelle

Rochelle Fisher FCIPD (she/her) | Deputy Director, Head of Senior Appointments and Talent

| Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office | PERSONAL |

text:
Email thread unit 6, message 1/6
Subject: USCAD contact
From: Robert Tinline[  →  To: JCS Nicholas
Date: 2024-12-21
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Subject: RE: USCAD contact


lain/Nick —
PSB the different buckets we propose sending papers under. We will have a fair
amount on the stocks which | can start sending across on Monday.

‘S —do we have a rough timeframe for Peter's vetting/clearance? Helpful to know
where we are at now so we can vet material accordingly.
Political/Congressional/Economic:

+ US politics scene-setter

+ Who's who in the incoming Trump Administration [list of personalities and bios]
+ How to best work with Trump and lessons learned from the first Trump
Administration

* Congress 101 [including bios of congressional leadership]

+ US economy overview [including expected economic policy of incoming Trump
Admin and latest US economy quarterly report as annex]

* State of the Democratic Party

+ Subnational engagement [overview of the consulates, our subnational
engagement, key organisations and figures]

• Overview of US media landscape
• Overview of main DC-based think tanks
Trade and Global Issues:
• Trade and tariffs
• Economic security
• AI and emerging tech
• Science and research cooperation
• Energy, climate and environment
• Agriculture
• Transport
• Business engagement
Foreign and Security policy:
• Middle East - Israel/Gaza, Syria, Normalisation
• Iran
• Russia/Ukraine
• Development, the UN and Human Rights
• China and Indo-Pacific, including DPRK
• The Americas
• Euroatlantic security
• Sanctions
• Cyber
• BIOT
• Justice and Home Affairs
• Illicit finance
• Counter-terrorism
• Special cases, privileges and immunities
Best wishes,
 | Chief of Staff to His Majesty’s Ambassador | British Embassy 
Washington
3100 Massachusetts Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20008 | Tel: 
 | Mobile: 
 | Email:

Hi Iain
James asked me to reply to you on this. I haven't seen the separate emailing so am coming 
to this a bit blind but we were working on the basis that 
 would send you a list of topics 
that we will cover today. I wasn't aware of the tight deadline for the wider pieces of work.

Nick

Email thread unit 10, message 3/3
Subject: Urgent vetting question
From: lan Collard*< ~  →  To: 4 NAL
Date: 2024-12-23
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Michael, Vincent's team,

Gerard McGurk was in touch last week about the expected (now confirmed) appointment of Lord Peter Mandelson
as the next UK Ambassador to Washington.

The specific question was whether Lord M is exempt from a UKV process (specifically for DV clearance) as a
member of the House of Lords?

We think CO say he is exempt. But we want to double check given the policy around public appointments, and
whether that alters the calculation?

He plans to begin duties (though not his posting) in early Jan, so time is of the essence.

Best wishes,

lan Collard | Chief Property & Security Officer
Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office
King Charles Street | London | SW1A 2AH

Dear JCS and colleagues,

Our FCDO Americas Director has told us there is a possibility that Lord Mandelson’s
DV interview tomorrow may be cancelled because UKSV require more information
on who he knows. Lord Mandelson will be looking to try to expand on this
requirement later this afternoon.

It remains important the interview happens tomorrow. There’s not another slot and
UKSV colleagues talking through with him during the interview will help get answers,
with the possibility of follow up at a later stage.

Could you confirm the DV interview will still proceed?

Thanks.

Gerard

subject: Personal/LPM
text:
Email thread unit 25, message 1/1
Subject: Personal/LPM
From: lan Collard  →  To: Corin Robertson  JCS
Date: 2025-01-30
Classification: none

Dear Corin, JCS (for Olly),

An update. This information is provided in strictest confidence, to respect vetting confidence
principles, because Corin and Olly are directly in my line management chain as Chief Security
Officer.

ESND have approved the DV clearance of Lord Mandelson. He was sent notification and his
DV certificate by email (on Osprey) on the evening of 29 January 2025. It will be valid for
five years.

As part of the usual clearance process, UKSV identified some areas in his application for
ESND to review:

* Tunderstand that Lord Mandelson’s private sector engagements are being managed by
HRD and Legal Directorate through the conflict of interest process.

¢ With regard to personal conduct, I understand that Lord Mandelson has received a
letter from Mervyn Thomas, informing him of his responsibilities as an FCDO
employee, including under the Diplomatic Service Regulations.

72

* Matters pertaining to his overseas contacts will certainly be reviewed by the STRAP
Authorities. *** , 1am unable to assure about the
decision they will take. Their decision will determine the access that Lord Mandelson
has to the most sensitive information and meetings.

ESND shared with Lord Mandelson (and separately with JCS in Americas
Directorate) the STRAP application forms, which we have asked Lord Mandelson to complete
as an urgent priority. We will seek to expedite the processing of his application as soon as the
completed forms are retumed to us.

Best wishes,

Tan

Ian Collard | Chief Property & Security Officer and Director Estates, Security &
Network

subject: Subject: Subject: FAC letter re HMA Washington
text:
Email thread unit 29, message 4/6
Subject: Subject: Subject: FAC letter re HMA Washington
From: Caroline Hurndall <  →  To: Dan York-Smith
Date: 2025-09-13
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Dan,  
Thanks for this. Team are drafting as we speak; Ailsa and I agreed early afternoon for 
a draft. I’ve asked for it to be based on - and not go further than - our public lines on 
vetting, and then to add some lines on appointment processes in order to gently push 
back on the idea that the FAC might be able to assist/assure our recruitment 
processes. Finally, it would include some lines referring to the fact that HMA Washington 
met the Foreign Affairs Committee on their visit to Washington DC in June 2025; further 
meeting was planned when HMA was in London later in June but had to be postponed due to 
scheduling changes.  
Do let me know if you think that sounds along the right lines, draft to 
follow. Best wishes  
Caroline  
Sent from Outlook for iOS

subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
text:
Email thread unit 30, message 3/3
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Jcs  →  To: Symes, Elkie - CO (OFF-SEN)
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

83

--- Resending to correct email ----  
Elkie,  
Grateful if you can commission CO/UKSV to input into an Annex that will be shared 
with the FAC in response to their attached letter re. Mandelson. Grateful if we can have 
input by 2pm today please as we are aiming for FS to issue the letter today. Given 
FCDO do not own the security clearance process, the Foreign Secretary will not want 
to take responsibility for responding to the FAC on these matters. We therefore 
propose the FS write a short cover letter and an annex (owned by the Cabinet Office) 
with responses to their questions.  
To support your teams, the questions are set out below and I have included where we 
think the FCDO does have agency to answer the questions are they refer specifically 
to FCDO/the FS.  
Annex A - Cabinet Office response to the FAC questions  
1. What security concerns were raised by the agencies undertaking security
clearance ahead of Lord Mandelson’s appointment? [For CO to answer]
2. What was the response of the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development
Office to any such concerns? FCDO advice was not sought, nor given.
3. Was it the assessment of the FCDO that any such concerns represented a
potential barrier to Lord Mandelson’s appointment? FCDO advice was not
sought, nor given.
4. Were any conditions imposed on the remit of Lord Mandelson as Ambassador
in order to mitigate any security concerns? [For CO to answer]
5. Was there a decision made to dismiss security concerns? Was such a
decision taken by the FCDO or by No. 10? If so, by whom? [For CO to answer]
6. If no decision was taken to straightforwardly dismiss security concerns raised
by the relevant agencies, was a decision made to suspend or alter: (a) the
usual security vetting requirements; or, (b) the usual timeline for vetting
procedures? [For CO to answer]
7. Was it the assessment of the Foreign Secretary that there was sufficient time
between the announcement of Lord Mandelson’s appointment in December
and the beginning of his tenure on 10 February to carry out full thorough
vetting? Yes. While an expedited clearance process is faster than a routine
clearance, it is not unusual to do so, and within the bounds of the vetting
process.
8. Did the usual vetting process for diplomatic appointments commence in
relation to Lord Mandelson before his appointment was made public? [For CO
to answer]
Kind regards,  
 
 | Deputy Private Secretary to the Permanent Under 
Secretaries, Sir Oliver Robbins and Nick Dyer | Permanent Under-Secretaries’ Joint 
Private Office  
 | King Charles Street | Foreign, Commonwealth & Development 
Office Email:
 | Mob: 
JCS
JCS
84

or attachments.

subject: Clearance issue
text:
Email thread unit 32, message 1/1
Subject: Clearance issue
From: lan Collard  →  To: Mark Power  Catherine-Naomi Jobshare
Date: 2025-09-15
Classification: none

Mark, Catherine,
An outstanding issue is whether ESND should lapse, suspend or withdraw
Mandelson’s DV clearance? While practically it will make little difference, because
Post have already removed his access to the embassy, taken his laptop, suspended
his FCDO email account and stopped sharing any official information, there is a
presentational aspect.
| can imagine, for example, a PQ that asks — presume you have taken his clearance
away from him now? (And suggestions of incompetence if we haven't.)
But | presume there are also employment law issues still at stake? Hence, | don’t
want ESND to act precipitously. Grateful for views. Including ultimately from Olly,
once we have considered advice for him.
Distinction (as | understand it) between different ESND options:
+ Lapse — someone no longer works for FCDO; we put the clearance into
abeyance (including on the official vetting system) and wouldn't confirm it is
extant anymore
+ Suspend — we have concerns about the clearance being active, and inform the
individual we are taking the clearance from them (with commensurate
implications) while concerns are reviewed
+ Withdraw — we completely remove the clearance, again ordinarily informing the

individual
Views welcome.
Thanks,
lan
lan Collard | Chief Property & Security Officer and Director Estates, Security & Network
Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office | Personal

Chief of Staff. Personal | Executive Officer Personal

87

Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: JCS  →  To: Simon Madden
Date: 2025-09-15
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Hi All,
Many thanks for your comments. Please see attached an updated version of the 
letter, this takes onboard your comments and also final checks within FCDO.
Ailsa – on your text for answer 4 to explain how we have handled:
•
Line on mitigations removed as if we aren’t talking about detail we
don’t need to reference mitigations.
•
Line on no ministerial role included in the cover letter.
Grateful if I can have final confirmation you are content (in particular 
No.10 and Cab Sec) and we will then put to the FS.
We are still working to get the letter out asap today, so grateful for quick 
responses (the next FS window to review will be 1500, after that it will be 
1600).
Kind regards,
 | Deputy Private Secretary to the Permanent Under-
Secretaries, Sir Oliver Robbins and Nick Dyer | Permanent Under-
Secretaries’ Joint Private Office
 | King Charles Street | Foreign, Commonwealth & Development 
Office
Please ensure items for the PUSs’ daily e-box reach PUS Action by 1400 clearly marked for 
the relevant PS. Our portfolios can be found here. Please note that we will not routinely 
keep a record of any emails or attachments.

Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Ailsa Terry  →  To: 
Date: 2025-09-15
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

On Mon, 15 Sep 2025 at 13:13, Ailsa Terry 
Dear Caroline
We are content with the draft as we saw it although we would like to answer 
question 4 with something closer to ‘There are strict confidentiality 
rules around sensitive personal information in the vetting process and as a 
result we do not comment on individual National Security Vetting cases and 
any mitigations employed around specific individuals. These processes are 
conducted entirely independently of Ministers and findings/details other 
than the outcome are not shared with them’.
But to ensure we are clearing the final doc, can you resend to this chain 
given there were further edits being made this morning?
Many thanks,
Ailsa

subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
text:
Email thread unit 33, message 6/20
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Tom Bramley  →  To: Elkie Symes
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Thanks for sharing and sorry to chip in on this too.
On National Security Vetting, I think it is worth being clear that while 
UKSV conduct the vetting checks and make a recommendation, vetting 
decisions are made by the sponsor department.
Would suggest the following addition to the cover letter (and then repeated 
im the questions):
"Separately from this there is a long-standing formal process for National 
Security Vetting which is undertaken independently by the UK Security 
Vetting Agency (hosted by the Cabinet Office) on behalf of individual 
departments and reporting back to them for decision."
Tom
Tom Bramley
Director, Security Policy and Strategy

text:
Email thread unit 33, message 8/20
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Simon Madden  →  To: 
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: none

On Sun, 14 Sept 2025 at 20:19, Simon Madden 
Hi Caroline,
Just to follow up on initial response from me.
The references in the draft letter are ok but I’d prefer it to say that 
No10 asked us to do this. We don’t usually play a part in diplomatic 
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
91


appointments.
On the Annex as I said just now it would be wrong to link our due 
diligence with security or vetting. So when you say FCDO didn ’t play any 
part and no security concerns were raised, we would not expect any 
security concerns to be flagged as that was not the due diligence we were 
doing nor are we qualified to make security judgements. So if those 
references in the annex could be amended they would be great.
As I said, we don’t want to give a misleading impression of our due dil 
exercise.
Thanks
Simon
Simon Madden
Director of Propriety & Ethics
| 70 Whitehall | London | SW1A 2AS

On Sun, 14 Sep 2025 at 20:04, Simon Madden 
Hi I have a problem. I cannot agree to a reference to our DD process 
being linked to raising or not raising security concerns. That was not 
its purpose. That woujd be misleading.
Our due diligence was on Mandelson generally using open source material. 
It was not a security vetting process and it offered no judgements. It 
was not specific to Epstein. This was in addition to usual pre-employment 
checks carried out by others.
The text we had before was what we are content with
Thanks
Simon

subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
text:
Email thread unit 33, message 20/20
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: unknown  →  To: 
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE
--- Resending to correct email ----

On Sun, 14 Sep 2025 at 10:08, 
> 
--- Resending to correct email ----
Elkie,
Grateful if you can commission CO/UKSV to input into an Annex that will be 
shared with the FAC in response to their attached letter re. Mandelson. Grateful 
if we can have input by 2pm today please as we are aiming for FS to issue 
the letter today.
Given FCDO do not own the security clearance process, the Foreign Secretary 
will not want to take responsibility for responding to the FAC on these matters. 
We therefore propose the FS write a short cover letter and an annex (owned by 
the Cabinet Office) with responses to their questions.
To support your teams, the questions are set out below and I have included 
where we think the FCDO does have agency to answer the questions are they 
refer specifically to FCDO/the FS.
Annex A - Cabinet Office response to the FAC questions
1.
What security concerns were raised by the agencies
undertaking security clearance ahead of Lord Mandelson ’s
appointment? [For CO to answer]
2.
What was the response of the Foreign, Commonwealth and
JCS
JCS
99


Development Office to any such concerns? FCDO advice was not

3. Was it the assessment of the FODO that any such concerns
represented a potential barrier to Lord Mandelson’ s
appointment?

4. Were any conditions imposed on the remit of Lord
Mandelson as Ambassador in order to mitigate any security
concerns? [For CO to answer]

5. Was there a decision made to dismiss security concerns?
Was such a decision taken by the FCDO or by No. 10? If so,
by whom? [For CO to answer]

6. Jf no decision was taken to straightforwardly dismiss
security concerns raised by the relevant agencies, was a
decision made to suspend or alter: (a) the usual security
vetting requirements; or, (b) the usual timeline for vetting
procedures? [For CO to answer]

7. Was It the assessment of the Foreign Secretary that
there was sufficient time between the announcement of Lord
Mandelson’ s appointment in December and the beginning of
Ais tenure on 10 February to carry out full thorough

8. Did the usual vetting process for diplomatic
appointments commence in relation to Lord Mandelson before
Ais appointment was made public? [For CO to answer]

Kind regards,
Jcs

| Deputy Private Secretary to the Permanent
Under-Secretaries, Sir Oliver Robbins and Nick Dyer | Permanent Under-
Secretaries’ Joint Private Office

Personal King Charles Street | Foreign, Commonwealth &

Development Office

subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
text:
Email thread unit 34, message 6/18
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Tom Bramley  →  To: Elkie Symes
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Thanks for sharing and sorry to chip in on this too.  
On National Security Vetting, I think it is worth being clear that while UKSV conduct the 
vetting checks and make a recommendation, vetting decisions are made by the sponsor 
department. Would suggest the following addition to the cover letter (and then repeated 
im the questions):  
"Separately from this there is a long-standing formal process for National Security Vetting 
which is undertaken independently by the UK Security Vetting Agency (hosted by the Cabinet 
Office) on behalf of individual departments and reporting back to them for decision."  
Tom  
Tom Bramley  
Director, Security Policy and Strategy

subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
text:
Email thread unit 34, message 8/18
Subject: URGENT - Reply to FAC re. Mandelson CO/UKSV input
From: Simon Madden  →  To: 
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: none

On Sun, 14 Sept 2025 at 20:19, Simon Madden 
Hi Caroline,  
Just to follow up on initial response from me.  
The references in the draft letter are ok but I’d prefer it to say that No10 asked us to do 
this. We don’t usually play a part in diplomatic appointments.  
On the Annex as I said just now it would be wrong to link our due diligence with security or 
vetting. So when you say FCDO didn’t play any part and no security concerns were raised, 
we would not expect any security concerns to be flagged as that was not the due diligence 
we were doing nor are we qualified to make security judgements. So if those references in 
the annex could be amended they would be great.  
As I said, we don’t want to give a misleading impression of our due dil 
exercise. Thanks  
Simon  
Simon Madden  
Director of Propriety & Ethics  
| 70 Whitehall | London | SW1A 2AS  
_______________________________________________________________________________

From: Simon Madden  →  To: 
Date: 2025-09-14
Classification: none

On Sun, 14 Sep 2025 at 20:04, Simon Madden 
Hi I have a problem. I cannot agree to a reference to our DD process being linked to 
raising or not raising security concerns. That was not its purpose. That woujd be 
misleading.  
Our due diligence was on Mandelson generally using open source material. It was not 
a security vetting process and it offered no judgements. It was not specific to Epstein. 
This was in addition to usual pre-employment checks carried out by others.  
The text we had before was what we are content with  
Thanks  
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
105

Director of Propriety & Ethics  
 | 70 Whitehall | London | SW1A 2AS

On Sun, 14 Sep 2025 at 19:50, Caroline Hurndall 
wrote: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE  
Ailsa,  
With apologies for the delay, please see attached a draft response to the FAC 
letter of 12 Sept, which I’ve also re-attached here for ease.  
The FS has seen this but not yet cleared it for release – you’ll see there are a couple 
of areas that she wants triple checked, so that we can be totally confident that we are 
releasing accurate information to parliament. If there are suggested changes from 
No10 or Cabinet Office colleagues, please send them back to me so I can incorporate 
and clear with her.  
As per my earlier email, you’ll see we’ve refocussed the letter to be based on FCDO 
process, but drawn on the info sent over by PET and CabOffice colleagues in terms of 
process, for which many thanks.  
Best wishes and no doubt speak tomorrow,  
Caroline 
Caroline Hurndall │PPS/FS │

text:
Letter unit 35
From: unknown  ()
To: 
Date: 2025-09-16
Subject: none
Classification: Secret

In discussion with Foreign Office, Cabinet Office and No10 officials, I have looked into whether the
correct process was followed for both the appointment of HMA Washington, Peter Mandelson, and the
process around your decision to withdraw him from post. As you know this was a political diplomatic
appointment, and therefore - as set out in the note from my predecessor, which you received before
making this appointment - requires the necessary security clearances and doing due diligence on any
potential Conflicts of Interest or other issues of which you should be aware.

In both the appointment and the withdrawal, I have seen no evidence that proper processes were not
carried out adequately. In my discussions with officials I have reviewed or discussed:

- the Propriety and Ethics open source due diligence that you saw as part of the appointment process:

- the post appointment vetting which was completed post announcement but before he took up post; and
- your decision to withdraw HMA Washington once the content of the emails was known and HMA
Washington was given the right to reply to his line manager, Oliver Robbins (FCDO Permanent Under
Secretary)

I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges.

[TAIL]

Elkie Symes

Principal Private Secretary to the Cabinet Secretary

M: Personal

70 Whitehall, London, SW1A 2AS

Ellen Atkinson CBE LVO
Acting Director GeneralPropriety and Constitution Group
Cabinet Office

Personal
Personal

text:
Letter unit 37
From: unknown  ()
To: 
Date: 2024-12-20
Subject: none
Classification: OFFICIAL

submitting Lord Mandelson as a 
priority candidate for Developed Vetting to the UK Security Vetting Agency.  
Clearance was duly given before he commenced his posting on 10 February 2025.  
The FCDO also immediately took on the task of working with Lord Mandelson to 
understand and mitigate conflicts of interest in advance of his departure for 
Washington. 
Taken together this has met all of the actions set out in my predecessor’s advice of 11 
November. 
Your decision to withdraw Lord Mandelson as HMA Washington was taken in light 
of your belief that his account of his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein at the time of 
appointment had been less than totally candid, and that his replies on questions posed 
by his line manager, the FCDO Permanent Under-Secretary, left open the possibility 
that further damaging revelations would ensue. The FCDO is now in correspondence 
with Lord Mandelson about the termination of his contract. 
The evidence I have reviewed leads me to conclude that appropriate processes were 
followed in both the appointment and withdrawal of the former HMA Washington. 
I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges. 
SIR CHRIS WORMALD KCB 
Cabinet Secretary and Head of the Civil Service 
120

text:
Email thread unit 39, message 1/3
Subject: Chronological CV
From: Peter Mandelson  →  To: Robert Tinline  lain
Date: 2024-12-31
Classification: OFFICIAL

To: Peter Mandelson , lain Griffiths
Subject: RE: Chronological CV


We can certainly look at how we construct a programme — some of the more mundane

domestic things and unclassified externals at the start. We've warmed people up so should be

able to put get a programme pulled together swiftly once we have our marching orders. We'll

see what we get back from the Foreign Sec's team on Thursday am. Happy new year ..Rob

Rob Tinline | Americas Director | Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office


From: Peter Mandelson

Sent: 31 December 2024 17:31

To: Robert Tinline ; lain Griffiths

Subject: Re: Chronological CV

| don't think we should overthink the speaking engagement. It's private panel not public, for half
an hour and very general.

How long does the security clearance take ? Would it be better to frontload the non classified
calls eg with ministers and some of the people | want to see like Simon Fraser, Peter Hill, ex
HMAs etc ?

Peter

From: Robert Tinline < Personal

Date: Tuesday, 31 December 2024 at 17:20

To: Peter Mandelson Personal , lain Griffiths

Subject: RE: Chronological CV


There'll be a ceiling on classification without clearance, but | don't think that's the primary issue.
The main question we're trying to clarify is around impact of the speaking engagement and the
delay to start date —_ if the logic for that is that you remain a private individual at the point of the
speaking engagement, how far is that consistent with beginning a full briefing round in advance
of it? Given the potential for him to have to explain our approach, the Foreign Secretary's team
need to tell us how they want to proceed.

Rob

Rob Tinline | Americas Director | Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office

123

From: Peter Mandelson 
 
Sent: 31 December 2024 17:03
To: Robert Tinline 
>; Iain Griffiths 
 
Subject: Re: Chronological CV
Edward and Gordon will get along famously.
I would much prefer to get going with calls etc w/b Jan 6 but will policy briefings/reading 
depend on vetting clearance ?

subject: Main thread - Senior Appointment
text:
Email thread unit 41, message 7/14
Subject: Main thread - Senior Appointment
From: Rochelle Fisher  →  To: 
Date: 2024-12-19
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Hi 
Colleagues may have a view on terminology but I think we are ‘ appointing’ , subject to 
the conclusion of bilateral processes.
Head of Mission roles are usually advertised across Government. The FCDO's 
recruitment processes are underpinned by the principle of selection for appointment 
on merit, on the basis of fair and open competition. Recommendations for 
appointment for these roles are ratified by the Foreign Secretary and, for the most 
senior posts, the Prime Minister. Appointments are then approved by HMTK.
The Constitutional Reform and Governance (CRAG) Act 2010 is the statutory basis 
for ensuring that Civil Service recruitment is fair, open and based on merit. Section 10 
(3) of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act allows for Head of Mission
appointments to take place outside of the CSC’ s rules and allows for political
appointments to be made to Head of Mission roles (or Governorships). Such
appointments are few in number but have been used by successive administrations
since 1945.
Best wishes
Rochelle
From: 
Sent: Thursday, December 19, 2024 3:58 PM
To: 
 <
>; Ailsa Terry (No.10) <
>; James Roscoe 
<
Philip R Barton <
 Robert Tinline 
<
Rochelle Fisher 
>; Janine Lloyd-Jones 
>
Cc: Caroline Hurndall <
Subject: RE: Main thread - Senior Appointment
You don't often get email from
Learn why this is important
Hi all – thanks for adding me. A few things that will help me on the comms below:
Please could someone share the correct terminology for the announcement – are we ‘appointing’ the 
next Ambassador or are we‘nominating'?
Some background on the usual process we follow for Ambassador appointments would be very useful too. 
Likewise, are there any previous examples of administrations making political appointments to Ambassador 
roles?
Thanks very much,
From: 
> 
Sent: Thursday, December 19, 2024 3:50 PM
To: Ailsa Terry <
 James Roscoe <
 Philip R Barton 
<

 Rochelle Fisher 
JCS

subject: HMA-designate Washington - draft Press Announcement
text:
Email thread unit 42, message 1/1
Subject: HMA-designate Washington - draft Press Announcement
From: UK HOM Postings{/  →  To: JCS
Date: 2024-12-24
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Attendees: Minister Vallance (PV), Lord Mandelson (PM) 
PM opening remarks 
� 
PM noted potential plans for an industrial strategy council and highlighted past 
experience with xWH initiatives of this sort after the 2008 crisis. PM added that 
he views the work on the S&T Framework as aligned with this approach to 
governance. 
� 
PM added that he hopes R&D spending won't come under pressure and isn't 
clear on whether or not this government will protect it in the same way as may 
have been done in the past. 
o
PV noted examples in the private sector that show maintaining R&D
spend is important even in challenging times.
� 
PM brieﬂy mentioned that the new CEO of UKRI, when appointed, should be 
working within direction/a policy framework from government. 
PV opening remarks 
� 
PV highlighted the importance of a joined up approach regarding procurement in 
relation to R&D spending, as this is important for company growth. 
DSIT work broadly. 
� 
PM noted the work of DSIT on being the digital centre of government and on AI. 
� 
PV mentioned that DSIT is to play a very important role in these areas among 
others. 
S&T Framework 
� 
PV noted that the S&T Framework is still important and PV hopes there will be 
xWH collaboration across the diﬀerent areas that S&T crosses over. PV added the 
importance of Prime Ministerial authority and a driver in the centre to ensure 
this, and brieﬂy referred to the Cabinet Committee on S&T.

Peter

From: 
 
Sent: 26 September 2024 11:20 
To: Minister JONES (DBT)
 
Cc:
 
 
Subject: RE: Invitation to roundtable 
Dear Sarah Jones MP, 
I hope you are well. 
Lord Mandelson has asked me to follow up with the invitation below to join a roundtable 
discussion. 
Please do let me know if you have any questions regarding the event or if I can help with 
anything. 
Kind regards, 
 
Global Counsel | Senior Executive Assistant 
 
From: Peter Mandelson 
 
Sent: 13 September 2024 13:05 
To:
 
Cc:
 
 
Subject: Invitation to roundtable 
Dear Sarah, 
I hope you are well and settling into your important new role. 
I am planning to reschedule the roundtable you agreed to participate in just before the 
election was called - this time focussing on how the Clean Energy Mission can be an 
important lever for building out our industrial strategy . And I wondered if you would be 
willing and available to lead off a discussion on the topic? SSE will also give some initial 
remarks. 
A number of industry people have highlighted to me that the new government's more 
ambitious decarbonisation agenda presents a major economic opportunity to onshore cable 
manufacturing, leverage the UK's geographical strengths in carbon capture and repurpose 
the UK's ports for offshore wind turbine making . The challenge will be identifying where the 
UK can compete with its international counterparts on green manufacturing and ensuring 
that policy and regulation choices develop these opportunities. In the absence of this, the UK 
risks not fully capture the benefits of decarbonisation. 
I am keen to bring together a group of interesting and, hopefully, lively people to discuss this 
opportunity and challenge. I would plan to invite a mix of industry, Labour MPs and policy 
people, senior union people and relevant influencers for what would be a Chatham House 
rule discussion. 
Third Party
Third Party
Third 
Party
Third Party
161


And of course, would be open to shaping the conversation according to your current focus in 
the departments. 
I would be pleased to speak with you about if further if useful. We are potentially looking at 
late afternoon (votes permitting) on Tuesday 10th or Wednesday 11th December – might 
either work with your diary? My office can come back with some alternatives if not. 
I very much hope you can do this, and it would be great to hear what you have to say. 
Very best, 
Peter 
Lord Mandelson 
President and Co-Founder 
A: 5 Welbeck Street, London, W1G 9YQ 
T:

“You will be accountable for good financial management and project delivery across

Sounds good JCS thanks. And no issues on the security side. We’ Il get to the FCDO reception for
1020 16 Jan. |’ Il confirm the team sheet shortly.
Just a thought, but Lord Robertson (as a member of the house of lords) hold parliamentary privilege
and so did not / does not require any vetting. He was granted Personal clearance on this basis (all
confirmed by the cabinet office). Essentially, it’ s the same as if he were a minister (they also don’ t
require SC/DV). All he needed was a STRAP brief. Might be worth exploring this avenue for Lord
Mandelson..?

STARTS 
Lord Mandelson, HMA-Designate Washington, wishes to meet Director GCHQ and CEO NCSC in 
January prior to his posting to Washington. He is eager to learn more about your cooperate with 
the US Intelligence Community and understand how he can best support your objectives from the

the FCDO. He intends to meet the other Agency Heads too. 
ENDS 
Many thanks,

Security Directorate |

so he should be able to stay with you until 1230.  
I think you’re aware that he has just met DG Q (at C’s encouragement), which he found very 
helpful.  
I should also mention, for transparency, that HMA-des is yet to receive his DV clearance. 
Given the short time frame until his posting, and the fact he is a Privy Counsellor, seniors 
here are giving him access to higher classification material on a case-by-case basis. I just 
wanted to ensure C and your other seniors are aware, but it is of course for them to judge 
what is appropriate, given this rather unique case.   
Kind regards,

--- READOUT ---

narrative/positioning around the US in the forthcoming Trade Strategy. LM and MA
then asked Private Office to leave the room so they could continue the rest of their
conversation politically.

text:
Letter unit 82
From: Lord Mandelson  ()
To: Secretary of State for Foreign
Date: 2025-02-14
Subject: none
Classification: none

In my first week as the British Ambassador to the US, | wanted to write to you to set out my
vision and ambition as | currently see it — for my role, for that of the Embassy, and for how |,
the Embassy in Washington and the whole of the US network work with Whitehall.

In order to deliver key aspects of the Prime Minister’s plan for change, and your six stated

priorities, my overarching goal is to refresh and revivify the basis of the UK's partnership
with the US. This partnership should recognise that we are

| would like the work of the Embassy and network to reflect these priorities, and for us to be
in a space to assist in shaping HMG’s current and future policy-making. To do this, the
Embassy's top team will work in a collective way with the whole government, just as!
expect HMG will want to do with us on US policy-making.

This approach will, however, also require trade-offs. In all the areas cited above, what

Thanks Ailsa, all
Some initial reactions to questions raised below:
Timing
 morning 1000EST/1500 GMT. The vote should take place 
in the first hour or so, with action on the 
. Exact timing will depend on speakers before and after 
the vote. And there’s scope for procedural wrangles (eg US asking to go first, or 
claiming the vote is void if the UNSC has acted; oral amendments from the floor; no 
action motions). We’ll keep you posted on the scenarios and will need instructions by 
1500 GMT including on potential EOVs.
The US have asked for the UNSC vote at 0900 EST (1400 GMT) on Monday.

subject: VISIT TO WASHINGTON - 27 FEBRUARY 2025
text:
Letter unit 91
From: Lord Mandelson  (British Embassy Washington)
To: Prime Minister
Date: 2025-02-24
Subject: VISIT TO WASHINGTON - 27 FEBRUARY 2025
Classification: none

President Trump has brought his particular stamp to the White House. America First
is the lodestar of his Administration. Policy decisions and approaches flow from that.
Over the next four years his deeply loyal and effective administration will play the
international system by a different set of rules — impacting trade, foreign policy, and
domestic nolicv with international implications. 8%

Trumn’s first month has seen his Administration *

|

H At his inauguration President
Trump said he intended to deliver on the promises made during his campaign,
especially on immigration, reform of the Federal government, and tariffs and trade
relationships. In the month since assuming office, his Administration has followed
through on this with amazing vigour.

277

subject: Steel/tariffs comms handling
text:
Email thread unit 124, message 3/4
Subject: Steel/tariffs comms handling
From: Jason Keen  →  To: Ailsa Terry
Date: 2025-03-11
Classification: OFFICIAL

Thanks @
 @Ailsa – there are three points where I would expect Ministers and the PM to be 
pushed beyond what we already have below:
• You aren’t retaliating now but why aren’t you retaliating now? Other countries
are.
We are taking a pragmatic approach in seeking a wider economic deal. A trade war is in nobody’s 
interests.
We will keep all options on the table, but our focus is securing a deal that will benefit industry and the 
British people.
• Isn’t this a pretty weak response?
I wouldn’t accept that. Other countries have also decided not to impose immediate retaliatory tariffs 
[WE THINK] and we want to focus on a long term economic deal.
We will always act in the best interests of the British people and economy.
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
JCS
Per
JCS
JCS
JCS
367


• We’ve heard a lot about PM’s great relationship with POTUS – but they’ve
spoken and Trump still ignored him. Not great is it?
Can we make the argument that the PM succeeded in his White House visit in getting an economic 
deal on the table –  which we hope to exempt steel/aluminium when it comes to pass –  and these 
global tariffs have arrived as we work together towards that?
Would welcome wider thoughts.
JK

JCS team,
Please find attached HMA's scenesetter ahead of the Secretary of State's visit to DC.
lam copying the travelling party and Ailsa.

We look forward to welcoming SoS and delegation on Monday.

Best,

JCS

Counsellor for Trade Policy, Economic Security and Agriculture
British Embassy, Washington DC
Cell: Personal

Follow us online: www.gov.uk/fcdo

This email is intended for the addressee(s) only: All messages sent and received by the Foreign,
Commonwealth & Development Office may be monitored in line with relevant UK legislation


388

15 March 2025 
The Rt. Hon Jonathan Reynolds MP 
Secretary of State for Business and Trade 
Dear Jonny, 
Scenesetter: your visit to Washington DC, 17-18th March 
Your visit will demonstrate UK commitment to advancing the Prime Minister and President’ s 
economic and technology deal. It comes at a critical time after US tariffs on steel and aluminium and 
ahead of expected aggressive “reciprocal” tariffs on 2 April. Sectoral tariffs, e.g. on pharmaceuticals, 
semi-conductors, autos, are also coming down the track. 
This is not just about “equalising” America’ s trade relationships through addressing tariffs and non-
tariff barriers. The America First policy aims to rebuild domestic manufacturing and create a new 
global trade order with allies working closely together (following US rules) that leaves China 
increasingly on the “other side of the fence”.
From the Ambassador  
The Rt Hon Lord Mandelson PC 
His Majesty’ s Ambassador 
3100 Massachusetts Ave, NW 
Washington, DC  20008 
Tel:

subject: Potential visit to DC w/c 9th June.
text:
Email thread unit 145, message 3/3
Subject: Potential visit to DC w/c 9th June.
From: Kumar lyer  →  To: Peter Mandelson
Date: 2025-03-22
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Dear Peter,
I have recently been confirmed as the UK’s next Ambassador to the UN & WTO in 
Geneva (a bit more detail here) and will be visiting Barbara and the team in New York 
w/c 9th June. I wanted to get your advice on whether it could be possible to visit 
Washington at the same time?
As with most things across the world at the moment, the US is key to the changes we 
are about to see across the multi-lateral system and it would be genuinely valuable to 
speak to those aligned to our views (e.g. on the Hill and think tanks) as well as those 
less so before I take up my role in the summer.
I appreciate how busy things must be so completely understand if this isn’t possible 
but if it is then please do let me know who I might be able to work with at the mission.
With thanks,
Kumar
________________________________________________________________________________
___________
Kumar Iyer CMG
Director General
Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office | King Charles St | London | SW1A 2AH
Tel: 
 | Email:
432

subject: State visit
text:
Email thread unit 154, message 1/1
Subject: State visit
From: Peter Mandelson  →  To: Morgan McSweeney, Oliver Robbins
Date: 2025-03-30
Classification: none

Dear Morgan, dear Olly,
I am glad we are now settled on September for the State Visit. The dates 17-19 September 
work well in a busy ‘back to work’ month in the US (and with UNGA High Level Week 
following on 23/9, and the party conference starting later on 28/9).
I am visiting the WH tomorrow, Monday, to meet with Susie Wiles and I know they are 
anxious to confirm SV dates. Are you content for me firmly to offer the September dates and, 
if acceptable to the President, for us to proceed quickly to the more formal offer? My team 
will also work up with No10/FCDO any follow up points for the Wiles meeting coming out of 
today’s PM-POTUS call.
 
 
 
***
***
***
454


 
 
 
 
 
Very best
Peter
Lord Mandelson
HM Ambassador, Washington DC
+
 | 
***
455

Peter
Specifically on the State Visit. | think for todav vou probably can go no further than the
PM/POTUS call, . You'd be saying

something like:
* Delighted it seems that we are aligning on an historic state visit in September.
The Palace is just working through the options in The King’s diary and then BE
Washington should be in a position to check the President's availability for
specific dates in the comina davs.

+ At our level we should focus on the content and narrative of the Visit. Would it
work for me to come to see you again next week to start working through this
given we will need pace and ambition from both sides to achieve our leaders’
aspirations?

Call if you need to discuss any of this. Hopefully we will soon be able to move from
working out the date to making a success of this important event. But | think

Olly

transformative or not.

Depending on what happens in the markets, the political marketplace might follow.

Peter

subject: Russia/Ukraine: London Talks 23 April
text:
Email thread unit 190, message 2/2
Subject: Russia/Ukraine: London Talks 23 April
From: JCS  →  To: Jonathan Allen
Date: 2025-04-21
Classification: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Jonathan

You and copy addressees might welcome a brief update on our plans to host the
next round of Russia/Ukraine talks in London on Wednesday (23 April), following
on from the meetings in Paris on Thursday.

We have moved fast over the Easter weekend to confirm that the meetings will take
place at 1 Carlton Gardens from c1 000 until 1600 on Wednesdav.

The structure of the day is coming together, but still a bit fluid, as we pin down
arrival details of some of the parties. =

On the policy, we have worked up, or commented on several supporting papers,

which we will share on a higher tier once finalised. 
There will be a media 
trail overnight Tuesday.
 OBE | EECAD region out-of-hours Senior Point of Contact | Eastern 
Europe and Central Asia Directorate| Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office | 
Email: 
| Tel: 
 | Mobile: 
***
***
JCS
JCS
545
